food_subgroup: Pasta dishes with sauce and optional protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for spaghetti with red tomato sauce and optional chicken or vegetable protein. FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pasta as a category. No specific code exists for spaghetti with tomato-based red sauce and optional chicken or vegetables in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 (pasta dish) is the closest available parent concept. No specific code for spaghetti with red sauce and optional chicken/vegetable protein.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spaghetti with red sauce is approximately 350 g, comprising cooked pasta, tomato-based sauce, and either chicken pieces or mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti with tomato sauce (cooked) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry covers spaghetti with tomato-based red sauce and optional chicken or vegetables as served in Singapore. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 168936), canned tomato sauce (FDC ID 170457), and cooked chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), combined in proportions typical of a restaurant pasta dish (approximately 55% pasta, 30% tomato sauce, 15% chicken/vegetables by weight). The vegetarian variant substitutes mixed vegetables for chicken; macros are similar. Values represent a blended per-100g estimate for the mixed dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — spaghetti, white, boiled
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 45 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white spaghetti, boiled (GI range 35–55, representative value 45). The tomato-based red sauce and optional chicken or vegetables do not materially raise the GI of the dish. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 22.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 35.4. Serving size used: 350 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to manage sodium intake from this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (22.5 g per 100 g) with a low glycemic index of 45; a full serving provides approximately 78.75 g of carbohydrate, which requires careful portion management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in a meal plan given its low GI, but should limit portion size to approximately 150–200 g to keep the glycemic load within a manageable range.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content (0.6 g per 100 g) and tomato-based sauce providing lycopene and antioxidants; however, sodium remains a concern for those with concurrent hypertension.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is relatively heart-friendly due to its low saturated fat profile, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the vegetarian variant and request low-sodium preparation to further reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti with Red Sauce (Vegetarian or Chicken) is a Western-style pasta dish served in Singapore, consisting of al dente spaghetti noodles coated in a tomato-based red sauce, available in a vegetarian version with mixed vegetables or a chicken version with sautéed or grilled chicken pieces. The dish is prepared by boiling spaghetti until cooked, then combining it with a sauce made from tomatoes, garlic, onion, olive oil, and herbs such as basil and oregano. The flavour profile is mildly tangy and savoury with a soft, slightly chewy texture from the pasta. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at casual Western restaurants and café-style eateries, often served as a main course. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (approximately 142 kcal per 100 g), a balanced macronutrient profile with carbohydrates as the primary energy source, and a low glycemic index of approximately 45, making it a relatively moderate-GI meal. The tomato sauce contributes lycopene, vitamin C, and potassium. The chicken variant adds lean protein. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is suitable for halal diets and is gluten-containing due to wheat-based pasta.
common_user_queries: Is spaghetti with red sauce healthy?
How many calories in spaghetti with tomato sauce?

Can I eat spaghetti if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of pasta with tomato sauce?

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific restaurant dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked spaghetti, tomato sauce, and chicken breast in proportions typical of a restaurant serving. GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for white spaghetti. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked spaghetti FDC 168936, tomato sauce FDC 170457, chicken breast FDC 171477) blended in approximate restaurant proportions. 2. The dish is listed under 'Indian' cuisine in the field survey source, but the food itself is a Western-style pasta dish; cuisine has been corrected to 'Italian-influenced / Western' to reflect the actual food identity. The Indian cuisine tag likely reflects the restaurant's broader menu category at Dugout - Circular Road. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a home-style pasta dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA component data. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimate. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for white spaghetti, boiled. 6. The vegetarian and chicken variants are treated as a single record with similar macronutrient profiles; the chicken variant has marginally higher protein. 7. Serving size of 350 g is a typical Singapore restaurant portion for a pasta main course. 8. Allergen note: contains gluten (wheat pasta); may contain dairy if cheese is added as a garnish, though the base dish is dairy-free.
